entry.title: SAP BTP Trial phone verification blocked - not allowed to request phone calls
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I am trying to activate my SAP BTP Trial account, but I cannot complete the telephone verification step.

After entering my phone number, completing the CAPTCHA, and clicking “Request Code”, I receive this error message:

"We did not send you a code because you are not allowed to request phone calls. Please contact your system administrator."

This is a personal/student SAP BTP Trial account, not a company-managed account.

I already contacted SAPBTPTrialSupport@sap.com and sso@sap.com, but I received an autoreply asking me to check SAP Community.

Could someone from SAP please advise how I can unblock the phone verification or manually verify my account?

Country: Tunisia
SAP User ID: P2012826210

Thank you.
Best regards,
Adam El Hajri

entry.title: SAP Joule for Materials Management (MM) in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
entry.content:
Hello, and thank you for taking the time to read this blog post!

As a member of the Global Delivery Hub SCM LAC Team, it is important to share experience with the SAP community and make everyone aware of the available features in the SAP system.

In this post, we provide a structured overview of what SAP Joule can do today for Materials Management (MM) in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition covering master data and procurement.

Master Data — Business Partner

Joule covers the full Business Partner (BP) lifecycle.

For creation, two modes are available: a guided step-by-step flow and a single-prompt mode where all details — BP type, grouping, name, address, and contact data — are provided at once. Both models include automatic duplicate checking and offer three outcomes: direct creation, an MDG governance draft requiring workflow approval, or cancellation.  Available on 2021+ (no MDG); with MDG, requires 2023 FPS1+.

 

For display, Joule retrieves overview, basic data, standard address, bank details (with validity date filters), identification numbers, roles, payment cards, industries, relationships, and address usages — all via natural language. This is supported in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2023 or higher releases. In case of 2021, a few capabilities may not be completely supported.

 

 Search supports filters by address, payment card, name, identification, tax data, bank account or IBAN, and communication details. It is available for 2021+.

 

Editing covers name, birthdate, organization data, communication details, search terms, legal form, authorization group, and natural person flag, plus the ability to add new addresses, identification records, and bank accounts. It is available for 2021+.

 

Joule also manages BP Certificates: viewing, exempting, extending validity, and listing BPs with missing mandatory certificates for a country.  It is available for 2023 FPS04+.

Master Data — Supplier and Customer

For both Suppliers and Customers, Joule supports display and search using natural language. Display covers account group, company code, posting block status, withholding tax details, partner functions (Payer, Ship-to Party), and purchase or sales organizations. Search accepts filters by account group, company code, purchase or sales area, city, country, and creation date range. Count queries are also supported — for example: How many suppliers of account group SUPM were created between 01.01.2024 and 30.06.2024?

They are available for 2021+.

 

 

Master Data — Product Master

Using the role SAP_BR_PRODMASTER_SPECIALIST, Joule retrieves MRP and supply planning fields including lot size procedure, MRP type and group, safety stock, reorder threshold, planned delivery duration, planning strategy group, availability check type, and goods receipt duration for a given material and plant.

It is available for 2021+.

 

Procurement

Operational purchasers can retrieve Purchase Order and Purchase Requisition details directly through Joule using filters such as document ID, purchasing organization and group, supplier, material, plant, delivery status (including overdue and in-approval), currency, and creation date range. PR queries additionally support filtering by total value and by the name of the person who created the requisition.  It is available for 2021+.

 

 

For Purchase Requisition creation, Joule supports four item types — free-text material, free-text service, codified material, and codified service — with optional source of supply in all cases. The interaction can start with a simple Create purchase requisition prompt or include all required fields in a single message. It is available for 2021+.

 

Mass delivery date updates across purchase order schedule lines are also supported, for both a single PO and multiple POs simultaneously (filtered by company code, supplier, current date, and new date). Joule can additionally display the workflow approver for a given purchase order. It is available for 2021+.

 

The Returns Purchase Order flow covers the end-to-end process: the Warehouse Clerk analyzes dead stock or slow-moving materials, searches for source POs, and creates a returns PO on hold; the Purchasing Analyst then verifies and releases the return. Joule navigates both personas with prompts like Analyze dead stock to return or Complete the returns purchase order 4500012535. It is available for 2025 FPS01+

The Quotation Comparison and Awarding capability enables users to view supplier quotations, compare them, get the best bidder suggestion, and award the supplier directly from Joule.

It is available for 2022 FPS00+ and 2020 FPS01+.

 

 

Key Takeaway

Across master data and procurement, Joule enables users to interact with business data through natural language, with no transaction code knowledge required and no change to the existing authorization model — Joule respects the same role-based access already configured in your system.

If you’d like additional information about the features, please refer to the Help documentation at the link Release-Specific Capabilities | SAP Help Portal.

The Global Delivery Hub SCM LAC Team will continue publishing posts with demonstrations for each of these areas.

Thank you for reading — if you found this post useful, please consider giving it a Like or leaving a comment with topics you would like us to cover next.

entry.title: Getting Started with GitHub in SAP Business Application Studio for SAP UI5 Development
entry.content:
Overview:

SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) trial accounts expire every 90 days, making it essential to safeguard SAP UI5 projects using a reliable version control system. GitHub not only helps prevent project loss but also introduces the collaboration practices widely used in professional software development.

This blog covers:

  • Connecting SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) with GitHub
  • Step-by-step process to push and manage SAP UI5 projects using GitHub.

The intent is to provide a simple and practical guide for SAP UI5 learners and developers to protect their projects, understand Git and GitHub fundamentals, and adopt industry-standard version control practices.

Introduction:

When I started learning SAP UI5, I was using a trial account on SAP Business Application Studio (BAS). 

Everything was going great — until I hit my first big obstacle. 

  • Every 90 days, the trial account expires. And when it does, everything is gone. All my projects. All my code. Vanished. 

My only option back then was to manually download each project as a zip file to my desktop before the account reset. Then when the new trial started, I had to upload everything back again. 

It was painful. It was repetitive. And one time, I forgot — and lost a project I had worked on for weeks. 

That's when I discovered GitHub — and it completely changed how I work.

I started learning how to:

  • Upload my SAP UI5 projects from SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) to GitHub.
  • Access them anytime, from anywhere, even after my BAS trial account resets.
  • Share my projects easily with teammates and collaborate more effectively.
  • No more downloading ZIP files. No more worrying about the 90-day deadline. My code was safely stored in the cloud, always accessible, and backed by version control.

What Is GitHub and Why Do We Need It?

The easiest way to think about GitHub is this: it's like Google Docs for source code. Instead of collaborating on documents, developers collaborate on code while Git keeps track of every change.

GitHub is a cloud-based platform built on top of Git, a distributed version control system. It enables developers to store their source code in the cloud, track changes over time, and collaborate with others while maintaining a complete history of every modification.

In addition to version control, GitHub provides features such as repositories, branches, pull requests, and code reviews, making it easier for individuals and teams to manage software projects efficiently.

Although GitHub is widely used for open-source development, it is equally important in enterprise environments. Development teams use it to collaborate on projects, review code, manage changes, and maintain high-quality software throughout the development lifecycle.

GitHub is not just a tool for beginners. It is an industry-standard platform used by organizations of all sizes to support real-world software development and team collaboration.

In real-world software projects, developers rarely work alone. Front-end developers, backend developers, testers, and other team members often work on the same application simultaneously. Without a version control platform like GitHub, collaboration can quickly become difficult, with developers accidentally overwriting each other's changes and having no clear way to track who changed what or revert to previous versions.

GitHub solves this challenge through features such as branching. Think of the main project as a tree trunk, where each developer creates their own branch to work independently on a feature, enhancement, or bug fix. Once the work is complete, the changes are reviewed through a Pull Request (PR) and then merged back into the main branch.

This approach enables multiple developers to work in parallel without interfering with one another, maintains a complete history of code changes, and ensures that every update is reviewed before becoming part of the main project. As a result, GitHub has become an industry-standard platform for version control and team collaboration in professional software development.

A Typical Team Workflow
A typical GitHub workflow in a development team looks like this:

 
main branch          → Stable, production-ready code

├── developer-1      → Working on the Login feature
├── developer-2      → Working on the Dashboard feature
└── developer-3      → Fixing a bug in the Search feature

Each developer works on their own branch without affecting the work of others. Once a feature or bug fix is complete, the developer creates a Pull Request (PR) to request that their changes be reviewed before they are merged into the main branch.

A senior developer or technical lead reviews the code, provides feedback if any changes are required, and approves the Pull Request once the code meets the project's quality standards. After approval, the changes are merged into the main branch.

This workflow helps ensure:

  • Developers can work independently without interfering with one another.
  • Every code change is reviewed before being merged.

A complete history of changes is maintained, making it easy to track who changed what and when.

The main branch remains stable and ready for deployment.

 Now, let's walk through the step-by-step process of connecting SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) to GitHub and uploading your SAP UI5 project.

Step- 1: Create a GitHub Account

  • Sign up for a GitHub account if you don't already have one.

Step 2: Initialize the Git Repository

  • Open your SAP UI5 project in SAP Business Application Studio (BAS).
  • Open the Terminal by navigating to Terminal → New Terminal.

    Run the following command to initialize a Git repository with main as the default branch:
git init -b main​

This command initializes a new Git repository in your project directory and creates the default branch named main. Git will now start tracking changes made to your project.

Step 3: Stage All Project Files

  • After initializing the Git repository, stage all the project files by running the following command in the terminal:
git add .​
  • The git add . command stages all the files and folders in the current project directory. Staging tells Git which changes should be included in the next commit.
  • Once the files are staged, Git is ready to create a snapshot of your project using a commit.
  • Note: The . (dot) represents the current directory, so git add . stages all files within your project.

Step 4: Commit the Changes

  • Once all the project files have been staged, create your first commit by running the following command:
git commit -m "Vendor Details"​
  • Creates a commit with the message "Vendor Details", which serves as a snapshot of your project at its current state.
  • Verify the Commit
  • To confirm that the commit was created successfully, run the following command:
  • git log
  • Displays the commit history of the repository, including the commit ID, author, date, and commit message.
  • If the commit was successful, you should see an output similar to:
commit 3f2c9b8e6d4f1a2b...
Author: <Your Name> <your-email@example.com>
Date:   Sat Jun 6 10:30:15 2026

    Vendor Details​
  • This confirms that your project has been committed successfully and Git is now tracking this version of your code.

Step 5: Configure Git (First-Time Setup)

  • If you're using Git for the first time, configure your username and email address using the following commands:
git config --global user.name "<github_username>"
git config --global user.email "<github_email>"
  • Configures your Git username and email address. These details are associated with every commit you create.

Step 6: Create a GitHub Repository

  • Sign in to your GitHub account.
  • Click New Repository.
  • Enter a repository name (for example, Vendor_Details_App).
  • Click Create repository.

Step 7: Connect the Local Repository to GitHub

  • After creating the repository, copy its HTTPS URL and run the following command in the BAS terminal:
git remote add origin https://github.com/sap-honey/Vendor_Details_App.git
  • Connects your local Git repository to the remote GitHub repository.

Step 8: Push the Project to GitHub

  • Push your local commits to the remote GitHub repository using the following command:
git push -u origin main​
  • Uploads your local main branch to the remote GitHub repository. The -u option sets the upstream branch, so future pushes can be performed simply by running:
git push​

  • When you run the git push command for the first time, GitHub prompts you to authenticate your account.

  • Click Copy and Open GitHub.

  • This copies the verification code and opens the GitHub device activation page in your browser.
  • GitHub automatically opens the device activation page. If prompted, sign in to your GitHub account.
  • Paste the verification code and click Continue, then click Authorize to grant SAP Business Application Studio permission to access your GitHub repository.
  • After the authorization is complete, return to SAP Business Application Studio. The git push command will finish automatically.
  • Open your GitHub account and navigate to the repository you created.
  • Refresh the repository page. You should now see all your SAP UI5 project files successfully uploaded.

    This confirms that your local SAP UI5 project has been successfully pushed to GitHub. From now on, you only need to commit your changes and run:

git push​

GitHub is an essential tool for every SAP UI5 developer. By connecting SAP Business Application Studio with GitHub, you can securely store your projects, track changes, and continue your work even after a BAS trial reset.

I hope this blog helps you get started with GitHub and makes managing your SAP UI5 projects easier and more efficient. Happy coding!

entry.title: DSP Models used in SAC Stories (BW-like where-used)
entry.content:
Having difficulties to identify impact of failed DSP Loads to SAC Stories? 
Having difficulties to identify how many SAC Stories need to be adjusted with an DSP Model Dimension change for example? 
Looking for where-used like BW feature? Impact analyzes? Lineage from DSP to SAC. 

As of Today, there is no automated, 100%, way to mass identify DSP models in SAC Stories.

Manual Steps:
1. Get all Stories and all Models data into a JSON: https://<your-tenant>/api/v1/stories?include=models
2. Save the results into a file
3. Process the file with a parser

I used python and have had the script vibe-code generated: I asked: "create parser python script on an example (first Story from the JSON) with delimiter: "{"name":" to "{"name":" and output format for (for example) DevOps Wiki. Provide a List of Models for each Story (use models: and description:)_. Can be enhanced with: create link to SAC Story providing the base-link as well.

Can be automated, but... bearer token is needed to fetch the result using a notebook, can be downloaded from browser, but SAC has only 8 hours validity. Since the Story to Model is static, manual way should be enough.

Simple and efficient. API: /api/v1/stories?include=models helps not only to create dynamic jump links to another SAC Stories, but also can be used for semi-automated documentation

Example: 

{"name":"<STORY NAME>","description":"<STORY DESC>","createdBy":"<USER_ID>","id":"<STORY_ID>","created":"<TIMESTAMP>","changed":"<TIMESTAMP>","isTemplate":false,"isSample":false,"models":[{"description":"<MODEL1_ID>","id":"<MODEL1_INTERNAL_ID>","remoteConnection":{"connectionType":"DIRECT","systemType":"DWC","name":"<CONNECTION_ID>","protocol":"HTTPS","host":"<URL>","port":"443","id":"<CONNECTION_ID>","scpAccount":null},"isPlanning":false},{"description":"<MODEL2_ID>","id":"<MODEL2_INTERNAL_ID>","remoteConnection":{"connectionType":"DIRECT","systemType":"DWC","name":"<CONNECTION_ID>","protocol":"HTTPS","host":"<URL>","port":"443","id":"<CONNECTION_ID>","scpAccount":null},"isPlanning":false}],"changedBy":"<USER_ID>","openURL":"<URL>"},{"name":.... 

 

Main blog: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/bw-vs-datasphere-dsp-amp-sac/ba-p/1428...

entry.title: WE02 List Specific Segment – How to Display/Export Parent IDoc Number (DOCNUM) as a Column?
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I am using WE02 and the List Specific Segment function to display all records for a custom detail segment (Z1DCSCONSDET) across multiple IDocs.

My goal is to create a report/export that combines all detail segment records from multiple IDocs into a single Excel file while also identifying which parent IDoc each record came from. Ideally, the output would contain both the segment data and the corresponding IDoc Number (DOCNUM).

The report currently displays the segment fields correctly (Material, Batch, Quantity, Comp.Id, Posting Date, Header Text, etc.), but I do not see an option to add the parent IDoc Number as a column.

When I select a segment row, the status bar at the bottom displays something similar to:

IDoc 000000009300930 Segment 000002

This indicates that SAP knows the parent IDoc associated with each segment record, but I have not found a way to display or export DOCNUM in the output.

My IDoc structure is:

  • Basic Type: ZDCSCONS
  • Header Segment: Z1HDR
  • Detail Segment: Z1DCSCONSDET

Is there a standard way to display or export the parent IDoc Number (DOCNUM) together with the segment data in WE02 List Specific Segment? My end goal is to create a consolidated Excel report of multiple IDoc segments while retaining the corresponding IDoc Number for each record.

Any guidance would be appreciated.

Thanks!

entry.title: Re: Error RW602 during Periodic Valuation posting - how t...
entry.content:
Hello!

In the periodic valuation posting context, GL_ACCOUNT is a runtime field in the posting interface that the system fills automatically as the last step of account determination, right before the FI document is created. GL_ACCOUNT is one of the required fields inside the ACCOUNTGL parameter in the standard structure that every line item of an accounting document must carry.

When RW602 says "Required field GL_ACCOUNT was not transferred in parameter ACCOUNTGL," it means: the valuation run got all the way to generating a posting line, but when it tried to create that line's account symbol to an actual GL account number, the lookup came back empty/wrong.

Message RW602 is not exclusive to Periodic Valuation; it is a generic "required field missing" check reused across several SAP components. And because doesn’t carries any variables identifying the specific transaction or account symbol involved, below are example of approches to be used to find the root cause of the issue:

- Review the detailed posting log for the periodic valuation run to identify the specific contract, condition type and flow type associated with the failing line item.

- Trace the account determination chain for that line item: Condition Type - Flow Type - Account Symbol - GL Account

- Cross-check whether the account symbol used by the failing flow type had a corresponding entry in account determination for the relevant chart of accounts, company code.

- Confirm whether the failure was isolated to specific contracts, contract types, or valuation rules, to determine if the cause was a data issue (specific contracts) or a customizing issue.

- Confirm that the corrected flow type has valid debit/credit account symbols assigned, and that those account symbols contains a GL account for the applicable chart of accounts, company code

- Re-run the periodic valuation posting in test/simulation mode for the failed valuation execution to confirm the accounting document is generated correctly.

entry.title: Re: SQL Anywhere Problem with proxy
entry.content: If you have access to support, you might want to collect the crash dump from the diagnostic directory and open support case. It is most likely going to be found in %ALLUSERSPROFILE%\SQL Anywhere 17\diagnostic. Please refer to https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_SQL_Anywhere/61ecb3d4d8be4baaa07cc4db0ddb5d0a/813e27e76ce210148bcad4e46a79b31f.html?locale=en-US&state=PRODUCTION&version=17.0.01#remarks which outlines where the directory might be located.

entry.title: Second Last Name in Recruiting and Onboarding 2.0 – Impact on EC Integration and Rehire Matching
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

We are evaluating the possibility of adding a Second Last Name field in Recruiting and Onboarding 2.0.

As a global company hiring in approximately 10 countries, we need to balance local requirements with a consistent global design. In many Latin American countries, an individual's legal name includes two surnames, but Recruiting currently only captures the first surname. This can lead to candidates entering their information inconsistently, creating data quality issues and challenges when matching candidate records with former employee profiles.

Our main concern relates to rehire and Contractor conversion processes, where accurate profile matching is critical. At the same time, we want to avoid introducing a solution that could create unintended issues for countries where a Second Last Name is not commonly used.

Has any company implemented a dedicated Second Last Name field in Recruiting and/or Onboarding 2.0?

  • Did it have any impact on Employee Central integrations?
  • Did you experience challenges with rehire matching, duplicate profiles, or profile identification?
  • How did you handle countries where a second surname is not part of the standard naming convention?
  • What best practices have you adopted to manage two surnames consistently across Recruiting, Onboarding, and EC?

We would greatly appreciate hearing about your experiences, lessons learned, and any recommendations or best practices.

Thank you in advance for your insights.

entry.title: Re: Error related to period determination occurred while...
entry.content:
Hello!

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition provides two configurable fiscal year variant (FYV) templates:

  • SZ  the general-purpose configurable FYV template, used as the posting fiscal year variant for company codes and ledgers.
  • SA  “Configurable template FI-AA US”, a companion template intended specifically for Asset Accounting period control logic (e.g. US GAAP depreciation conventions).

Company code 3010 correctly posts under FYV SZ at the ledger level. However, Asset Accounting allows a depreciation area to be assigned a different fiscal year variant than the one used for posting, via the configuration activity “Specify Other Variants on Depreciation Area Level”. The depreciation areas relevant to asset class Z001 under accounting principle USAP (valuation views 0US_0001 and 0US_0016, both using depreciation key SUL3) post their period control logic against FYV SA - the template purpose-built for this scenario.

The issue is that FYV SA is not completed: its “Period Control (Fixed Assets)” table contains zero entries. Because no calendar-to-period mapping exists under SA for the relevant period control indicator, the system can’t translate the asset's transaction date (2026-07-08) into a valid posting period, and error AA613 is raised.

SAP note 3491616 for the same error and message pattern in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, states the cause as: “The customer is using fiscal year variant SZ/SA and has not fully completed the customizing.” SAP note 3199550 further confirms the underlying mechanism: a different fiscal year variant set at depreciation area level which does not have complete customizing.

 

Configuration check  findings based on print screens:

  • FY Variant assigned to Company Code 3010 (all ledgers):  SZ - assigned consistently across ledgers 0L, 2L, 3L, and 4G. No ledger points to SA.
  • Period Control (Fixed Assets) table for FYV SZ: Partially built: entries exist only for period control indicators 05, 06, 07, 08, 10, and 11.
  • Period Control (Fixed Assets) table for FYV SA (“Configurable template FI-AA US”): empty - 0 entries found. This template was not configured.
  • Depreciation key SUL3 - Phase 1 / Period Control Method 011:    Uses period control indicators 11, 06, and 01 - none of these is “SA”, ruling out this phase as the direct source of the error.
  • Asset Class Z001 - Valuation View settings: SUL3 is assigned under accounting principle USAP for two valuation views: 0US_0001 (Book Deprctn, useful life 20) and 0US_0016 (Cust.-spec., useful life 39).

 

Resolution:

- Open the configuration activity “Specify Other Variants on Depreciation Area Level” for chart of depreciation 1710, and confirm that the USAP depreciation areas used by asset class Z001 (0US_0001 and 0US_0016) are assigned fiscal year variant SA.

- Decide on one of the following options

Option A - Complete FYV SA: Build out the “Period Control (Fixed Assets)” table for FYV SA, covering every period control indicator used by SUL3 (and any other US GAAP depreciation keys in scope), each mapped across all twelve calendar months, following the same pattern already used to build out SZ (see indicator 11, replicated once per month).

Option B - Remove the depreciation-area override: Remove the SA assignment at depreciation area level so that the USAP depreciation areas fall back to FYV SZ. This is applicable if SZ's existing period control logic is confirmed to correctly represent the US GAAP depreciation.

 

References:

SAP Knowledge Base Article 3491616 - “AA613 Period determination is not possible on date &2 for period control &1” (SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition).

SAP Knowledge Base Article 3199550 - “Get error AA613 when display asset via T-code AW01N or other transactions.”

SAP Note 20017 - “EAA613: No period determination possible” (referenced within KBA 3199550).

SAP Knowledge Base Article 3536186 - “Error AA613 happens in Asset transactions” (referenced within KBA 3199550).

SAP Help Portal - “Defining Your Own Fiscal Year Variant” (SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition documentation).

entry.title: General Billing - Error with pricing
entry.content:
Hi Gurus,

I have an interface that posts billing documents from our POS system to SAP. For this process, we are using general billing via the function module GN_INVOICE_CREATE. The pricing procedure contains several condition types, including:

YPR0 – Price List. The condition value is retrieved from a condition table and is mandatory.
YPMP – Manual Price.
YDCM – Manual Discount. 

In the copying control from the Sales Document to the Billing Document, I am using Item Category DLN and Pricing Type = G, according to SAP Note 2867988. 

The issue is that the YPR0 condition is not being created during billing. As a result, the billing document is considered incomplete/erroneous and cannot be transferred to Finance. When I check the condition YPR0 in the document I get the following information: 

Has anyone encountered a similar requirement or faced the same issue? Any guidance or recommendations would be greatly appreciated.

-Italo

entry.title: Update Processor through FIN_WF_AGENT BADI for Verify General Journal Entries Workflow
entry.content:
Hi Community Members,

I have implemented the BADI - FIN_WF_AGENT using the Custom Logic FIORI app in order to update the processor for Verify General Journal Entries workflow/

My requirement is to be able to dynamically assign processors for Verify General JE Workflow requests. I have a CBO where the list of approvers are maintained for each of the submitter (here submitter is the one who creates the JE using the Verify General JE FIORI app).

In the Manage Workflow for Verify General JE, we have added a deadline option which trigger after 1 minute of Step. And the initial recipient is another user (of type BP employee). This user (let's say USER_APRV) is also listed in the Manage Teams & Responsibilities FIORI app.

Now, when a new request is raised, initially the processor is set as USER_APRV. However, as the deadline callback class is triggered after 1 minute, the BADI implementation is also triggered and the processor gets updated. Inside the BADI the "old_agent" is replaced by the "new_agent".  Currently, the "new_agent" value is hard-coded. However, the requirement is that the "new_agent" should be determined by selecting the appropriate approver from the designated CBO based on the submitter of the request.

In the ABAP call stack, I can see that the method "if_swf_flex_ifs_run_appl_step~on_deadline_action_callback" of the class "CL_FDC_ACCDOC_VER_FLEX_RUN_APP" is being called and it contains the  workflow task id ("IV_TASK_WI_ID") as an importing parameter to the class. This task id can be used to query the CDS C_GLJRNLENTRYTOBEVERIFIED  to get the Journal entry detail along with the submitter.

Here comes the issue! Since the task id is an importing parameter and local to the class, I cannot read it using a dirty assignment technique. Also, I have using several other class-methods / Function modules to get the work-item id from the stack or the memory but nothing was successful.

If anyone has had such a requirement and was able to achieve this, it would be really helpful for me if you can share your insights.

Thank you,
Sayak

SAP Business Workflow  SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition Finance

entry.title: Re: Solution Process Country/Region if Synched from SAP S...

In SAP help of SAP Cloud ALM there is below information:
As you can now only maintain country/region information at diagram level, the Country/Region field in the General Information section of a custom solution process is read-only.
The same field is not available when you create a new custom solution process, as it will get its country/region information from the diagrams located within it.

entry.title: MD04 计划订单转生产订单 消息号 CO684
entry.content:
成品和半成品都是按50策略执行生产方式，集中/汇总字段都选择1，成品生产方式是委外工单，发半成品出去，成品回来。半成品是厂内生产。

业务数据流程如下：

  • 销售创建成品销售订单需求数量100 （正常）
  • 需求到后，运行MRP，成品转成委外采购订单数量100 （正常） 
  • 半成品第一次在MD04将计划订单转生产订单，本次只生产50个（正常）
  • 半成品生产完成之后，根据委外流程发货给供应商，然后成品入库。销售单出库。出库50个（正常）
  • 第二次使用MD04对半成品将剩余的50数量，转成生产订单报错，错误CO684

entry.title: Principal Propagation from BTP Web App to S/4HANA via SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction

During a recent customer PoC, I worked on a scenario where a BTP-hosted web application needed to call an S/4HANA on-premise API — not as a generic service account, but as the actual logged-in user. 
This is where Principal Propagation becomes essential. Preserving user identity end-to-end isn't just a technical nicety — it matters for auditability, compliance, and access control. When S/4HANA receives a call as the real user, every action is traceable: security audit logs show who did what, authorizations are enforced per user, and there's no shared service account masking individual activity.

In this post, I share the configuration and lessons learned that made it work—from a BTP web application authenticated via SAP Cloud Identity Services, through SAP Integration Suite and Cloud Connector, all the way to S/4HANA, with user identity preserved throughout the entire chain.

Thanks to @cj_lo  for preparing Cloud Connector with S/HANA On-premise demo system, testing, and troubleshoot together.

Scenario

A simple web application is deployed on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry. When the user clicks a button, it triggers an iFlow on SAP Integration Suite that fetches data from S/4HANA. The S/4HANA system must receive the call as the authenticated user — not as a technical service account. 
While direct access to S/4HANA via a BTP Destination and SAP Cloud Connector is possible, many organizations choose to introduce a middleware layer to support centralized governance, security, monitoring, and error handling. Ultimately, the target architecture is customer-specific and should balance integration requirements, operational considerations, governance needs, and cost.

Browser (logged-in user)
  → BTP Web App (via Approuter + Cloud Identity Services)
    → BTP Destination Service
      → SAP Integration Suite iFlow
        → Cloud Connector
          → S/4HANA (on-premise, as the user)

Prerequisites & Scope

This article assumes the following components are already installed and configured:

  • SAP BTP Cloud Foundry environment
  • SAP Integration Suite
  • SAP Cloud Connector connected to the SAP BTP subaccount
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication (IAS) as the Identity Provider
  • SAP S/4HANA system accessible through Cloud Connector
  • User identities mapped between IAS and SAP S/4HANA

This article focuses on the principal propagation scenario from a SAP BTP application through SAP Integration Suite to an on-premise SAP S/4HANA system. Topics such as SAP Cloud Connector installation, SAP BTP onboarding and trust configuration, access control setup, certificate management, and SAP S/4HANA user provisioning are outside the scope of this article. If I were to cover all of them here, I would end up writing a book instead of a mini blog 😉

Relevant SAP documentation is listed in the References section at the end of this article.

Solution Overview

The solution relies on three key components working together:

ComponentRole
ApprouterHandles IAS login, holds the user's JWT token
BTP Destination (OAuth2JWTBearer)Exchanges the user's IAS JWT for an Integration Suite-scoped token using the PI Runtime service key
iFlowReceives the user-scoped token, calls S/4HANA via Cloud Connector with Principal Propagation

The critical insight is using OAuth2JWTBearer as the destination authentication type. This is the only type that correctly takes the user's IAS JWT and exchanges it at the PI Runtime token endpoint — preserving the user identity all the way to SAP Integration Suite.

Configuration

The BTP app consists of the following files — each is covered in the steps below:

your-app/
├── manifest.yml
├── package.json
├── xs-app.json
├── xs-security-ias.json
└── dist/
    └── index.html

Provision BTP Cloud Foundry Services

Before deploying the app, create the required service instances in your Cloud Foundry (CF) space.
Below using CF CLI, if you need a crash course using it, please check out this tutorial here.

Target your CF API endpoint and log in (in this example my region is jp10, yours could be different):

cf api https://api.cf.jp10.hana.ondemand.com
cf login

Create a Destination service instance (used by the Approuter to resolve BTP destinations), you can replace iflow-pp-destination-svc with service name of your choice:

cf create-service destination lite iflow-pp-destination-svc

Create an IAS (Identity) service instance using the xs-security-ias.json security descriptor, you can replace iflow-ias-svc with service name of your choice:

cf create-service identity application iflow-pp-ias-svc -c xs-security-ias.json

Where xs-security-ias.json contains:

{
  "xsappname": "iflow-pp-ias",
  "tenant-mode": "dedicated",
  "credential-type": "X509_GENERATED",
  "oauth2-configuration": {
    "redirect-uris": [
      "https://*.cfapps.jp10.hana.ondemand.com/login/callback?authType=ias"
    ],
    "post-logout-redirect-uris": [
      "https://*.cfapps.jp10.hana.ondemand.com/*/logout.html"
    ]
  }
}

credential-type: X509_GENERATED uses a certificate-based credential instead of a client secret — recommended for production.

Both service instances are bound to the app via manifest.yml:

applications:
  - name: poc-iflow-pp-fe
    memory: 512M
    buildpacks:
      - nodejs_buildpack
    services:
      - iflow-pp-ias-svc
      - iflow-pp-destination-svc

Ensure the User's Email Matches S/4HANA Identity

The Cloud Connector Subject Pattern (configured under Cloud To On-Premise → Principal Propagation) determines how the X.509 certificate CN is built. The default pattern is:

CN=${name}

${name} is sourced from the user_name claim in the PI Runtime token. During the OAuth2JWTBearer exchange, PI Runtime populates user_name from the email claim of the incoming IAS JWT — not from sub or any other claim.

So the identity that reaches Cloud Connector and ultimately S/4HANA is always the user's email address.
This behaviour is important because many troubleshooting efforts focus on the IAS sub claim, while principal propagation in this flow ultimately depends on the user's email address being propagated and resolvable by the backend system.

Important: Make sure the user's email address in IAS matches a user identity that S/4HANA can resolve — either via direct email-to-username mapping on the S/4 side, or as the logon name if S/4 uses email as the logon name format.
Depending on your identity governance requirements, the Cloud Connector Subject Pattern can use mapping logic beyond a basic CN. On the S/4HANA side, certificate-to-user mapping (CERTRULE) adds a second layer of control, allowing a certificate CN to be mapped to a different logon name. For complex scenarios, refer to the Cloud Connector Subject Pattern Syntax and S/4HANA Certificate-Based User Mapping documentation to design patterns that fit your enterprise architecture.

To verify what identity Cloud Connector is propagating, check CC Admin UI → Logging and Tracing — the outbound request will show the CN value used in the short-lived certificate.

Here's how my configuration looks like in Cloud Connector:

 Here's how rule-based certificate mapping looks like in S/4HANA - /ncertrule

Create Process Integration Runtime Service Instance

You probably has setup this one in order to trigger any published iFlow; or if you have one but has not included below grant type, then you can create a new one in BTP Cockpit → Service Marketplace → Process Integration Runtime:

  • Plan: integration-flow
  • Grant Types to enable:
    • client_credentials (for testing)
    • jwt bearer (the one that we need)
    • refresh_token
    • saml2 bearer
  • Create a Service Key and note down clientid, clientsecret, and tokenurl

Why PI Runtime and not IAS credentials? In my landscape setup, SAP Integration Suite only trusts tokens issued by its own OAuth authorization server (via the PI Runtime instance - current the SAP Integration Suite is authenticated via SAP Default idP). IAS tokens forwarded directly are not trusted by SAP Integration Suite unless full IAS-to-Integration Suite trust federation is set up. The PI Runtime service key is the standard supported path.

Configure BTP Destination

In BTP Cockpit → Connectivity → Destinations → New Destination:

FieldValue
Name<unique destination name, i.e. m25_iflow>
TypeHTTP
URLhttps://<your-cpi-host>/http/<your-iflow-endpoint>
Proxy TypeInternet
AuthenticationOAuth2JWTBearer
Client ID<clientid from PI Runtime service key>
Client Secret<clientsecret from PI Runtime service key>
Token Service URL<tokenurl from PI Runtime service key>

No additional properties required.

Common pitfalls:

  • Do not use OAuth2UserTokenExchange — it uses refresh_token grant internally which PI Runtime does not support
  • Do not use IAS credentials here — SAP Integration Suite will reject them
  • The destination name must exactly match what is referenced in xs-app.json (watch for underscore vs hyphen)

BTP App — Approuter Setup

The frontend app is a plain HTML file served by the SAP Approuter (@sap/approuter). No custom middleware needed.

package.json:

{
  "name": "ar-iflow-pp-fe",
  "dependencies": {
    "@sap/approuter": "^22.0.3"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "start": "node node_modules/@sap/approuter/approuter.js"
  }
}

xs-app.json — routes inbound requests to the BTP destination or local static files:
Also remember to double-check the Destination name here in case you're using different name.

{
  "welcomeFile": "/index.html",
  "authenticationMethod": "route",
  "logout": { "logoutEndpoint": "/logout" },
  "routes": [
    {
      "source": "^/user-api(.*)",
      "target": "$1",
      "service": "sap-approuter-userapi"
    },
    {
      "source": "^/m25_iflow(.*)$",
      "destination": "m25_iflow",
      "csrfProtection": false,
      "target": "$1",
      "authenticationType": "ias"
    },
    {
      "source": "^/(.*)$",
      "localDir": "dist",
      "authenticationType": "ias",
      "cacheControl": "no-cache, no-store, must-revalidate"
    }
  ]
}

The /user-api route uses the built-in Approuter user info service — no custom code required to read the logged-in user's details.

dist/index.html — the entire frontend in one file:

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8" />
  <title>Principal Propagation PoC</title>
  <style>
    body { font-family: Arial, sans-serif; max-width: 600px; margin: 40px auto; padding: 0 16px; }
    button { padding: 8px 20px; margin-top: 8px; cursor: pointer; }
    pre { background: #f4f4f4; padding: 12px; white-space: pre-wrap; word-break: break-all; margin-top: 8px; }
  </style>
</head>
<body>

<h2>Principal Propagation PoC</h2>

<h3>Logged-in User</h3>
  <pre id="userInfo">Loading...</pre>

<h3>Trigger iFlow</h3>
  <button onclick="callIflow()">Call iFlow (GET /m25_iflow)</button>
  <pre id="iflowResponse">Response will appear here</pre>

<script>
    // Fetch logged-in user info from built-in Approuter user API
    fetch("/user-api/currentUser")
      .then(r => r.json())
      .then(u => document.getElementById("userInfo").textContent = JSON.stringify(u, null, 2))
      .catch(e => document.getElementById("userInfo").textContent = "Error: " + e.message);

// Trigger iFlow via BTP Destination (OAuth2JWTBearer)
    function callIflow() {
      call("/m25_iflow");
    }

function call(url) {
      document.getElementById("iflowResponse").textContent = "Calling " + url + "...";
      fetch(url, {
        headers: { "X-Requested-With": "XMLHttpRequest" }
      })
        .then(async r => {
          const text = await r.text();
          try { return "HTTP " + r.status + "\n\n" + JSON.stringify(JSON.parse(text), null, 2); } catch (_) { return "HTTP " + r.status + "\n\n" + text; }
        })
        .then(body => document.getElementById("iflowResponse").textContent = body)
        .catch(e => document.getElementById("iflowResponse").textContent = "Error: " + e.message);
    }
  </script>

</body>
</html>

Deploy:

cf push

Assign ESBMessaging.send Role to Users (IAS Group Mapping)

In BTP Cockpit → Security → Role Collections → Create (e.g. CPIMessagingSend) :

Roles tab — add:

Application IdentifierRole TemplateRole
it-rt-<your-instance>ESBMessagingESBMessaging.send

User Groups tab — add:

Identity ProviderName
<your-ias-tenant>.accounts.ondemand.com — Business UsersIntegration_Senders

Make sure your users are members of the Integration_Senders group in IAS, and select Business Users (not Application Users) as the user type — logged-in app users are business users in IAS.

iFlow configuration summary: The iFlow uses an HTTPS Sender Adapter with User Role authorization (ESBMessaging.send) and an OData/HTTP Receiver Adapter configured for On-Premise proxy type with Principal Propagation authentication. See the Integration Flow section below for the full configuration.

Building Integration Flow

This section assumes you are familiar with creating integration flows in Cloud Integration of SAP Integration Suite.

The integration flow in this demo scenario is intentionally minimal — its purpose is to receive the inbound call from the BTP web app, forward it to S/4HANA via Cloud Connector with Principal Propagation, and return the response.

The iFlow contains the following steps:

  1. HTTPS Sender Adapter — receives the inbound HTTP request from the BTP Approuter
  2. Request-Reply step — forwards the call to the S/4HANA OData service
  3. OData V2 Receiver Adapter — calls S/4HANA on-premise via Cloud Connector

HTTPS Sender Adapter configuration:

FieldValue
Address/your-iflow-endpoint
AuthorizationUser Role
User RoleESBMessaging.send

OData V2 Receiver Adapter — Connection tab:

FieldValue
Addresshttp://<virtual-host>:<port>/sap/opu/odata/sap/<service-name>
Proxy TypeOn-Premise
AuthenticationPrincipal Propagation
Location ID<your Cloud Connector location ID>
Custom Query Parametersap-client=<client no.>

OData V2 Receiver Adapter — Processing tab:

FieldValue
OperationQuery (GET)
Resource Path<EntitySetName>
Query Options$top=10 (or as needed)
Custom Query Options<SAP Client Number>

The sap-client parameter must go in Custom Query Parameters (Connection tab). The Resource Path must have no leading slash — e.g. `A_GLAccountInChartOfAccounts`, not `/A_GLAccountInChartOfAccounts`.

Testing the Flow

With the web app deployed, open it in the browser. You will see the logged-in user's identity displayed (via the /user-api/currentUser endpoint exposed by the Approuter). Click Call via iFlow (GET /m25_iflow) button to trigger the integration flow.

A successful end-to-end flow will show the same user email across the BTP app, the Integration Suite message log, and the S/4HANA audit log. The sections below describe how to verify each layer.

Cloud Connector — Trace

If the call reaches the Cloud Connector but something goes wrong on the S/4HANA side, enable tracing in the Cloud Connector Admin UI:

  1. Go to Cloud Connector Admin UI → Logs and Trace → Trace (Debug level)
  2. Trigger the iFlow again from the web app
  3. View the trace (scc_core.trc) and search for the CN value — this confirms which user identity Cloud Connector placed in the short-lived X.509 certificate sent to S/4HANA

This is especially useful when the call appears to succeed from the Integration Suite side but S/4HANA returns a 401 or an authorization error — it lets you confirm the CN matches a user S/4HANA can resolve.

S/4HANA — ICF Trace (`/nsectrace`)

For the deepest level of verification, enable ICF tracing on the S/4HANA side to see exactly what the system receives at the HTTP layer:

  1. In S/4HANA, run transaction /nsectrace
  2. Navigate to ICF Service / Path and enable the trace for the relevant ICF service path (e.g. `/sap/opu/odata/sap/<service-name>`) and click Activate Trace
  3. Trigger the iFlow again from the web app
  4. Back in /nsectrace, click Deactivate Trace, and choose Show Trace — the trace output will show the inbound request headers including the ssl-client-subject field, which contains the CN from the Cloud Connector certificate

The CN value here is what S/4HANA evaluates for user mapping. If it does not match a known logon name, the call will fail with a 401 even if everything upstream is correct.

Key Takeaways

Principal Propagation is more than a technical configuration—it enables auditability, compliance, and least-privilege access control by ensuring S/4HANA receives requests as the actual business user rather than a shared service account.

The implementation approach depends on customer requirements and the overall solution architecture. Whether user identity is propagated through direct connectivity or a middleware layer, the objective remains the same: maintain end-to-end user context while balancing integration needs, governance, operational considerations, and cost.

One of the key lessons learned is that principal propagation depends on several components being configured consistently across BTP, Integration Suite, Cloud Connector, and S/4HANA. Even small trust or authorization gaps can prevent user identity from being propagated successfully, making end-to-end validation essential.

References

The following SAP documentation was useful while implementing and troubleshooting this scenario.

SAP BTP - Cloud Foundry Environment

  • Administration and Operations in the Cloud Foundry

SAP Cloud Connector and S/4HANA (ABAP)

  • Cloud Connector Documentation
  • Installation Guide
  • Managing Subaccounts
  • Principal Propagation Configuration
  • Configure Access Control (HTTP)
  • Configure Subject Patterns
  • Subject Pattern Rules API
  • S/4HANA / ABAP – Rule-Based Certificate Mapping (CERTRULE)

SAP Integration Suite

  • SAP Integration Suite Documentation
  • Cloud Integration Documentation

SAP Cloud Identity Services

  • Identity Authentication (IAS) Documentation
  • Destination Service Documentation
  • OAuth2JWTBearer Authentication

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud | Workspace and Tenant
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

Thank you for your support.

Step 2

Step 3

  • Added @Semantics.geoLocation.latitude/longitude annotations
  • Associated to I_Country for descriptive text

Step 4

  • Mapped dimension CDS to main cube CDS ZBIFNAARC005
  • Result: Location Dimension still not appearing in SAC model

entry.title: Subcontracting with Sales Order Stock (E) in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: Scope Item 7DW
entry.content:
Business Case

ABC Scooter Company is executing a Customer Order of 100 Scooters with Matte Black Panel. The Body Panel is manufactured specifically for the specific customer order (Sales Stock E), while Primer and Paint are standard consumables issued from Regular Plant Stock. The subcontractor paints the body panel and returns the finished Painted Black Body Panel back into Sales Order Stock (E).

By using Sales Order Stock (E) in S/4HANA Public Cloud, the company ensures that all materials and subcontracting activities are strictly tied to the individual customer order, enabling precise cost tracking, eliminating cross-consumption.

Other applicable industries:

  • Industrial Machinery (custom components outsourced for specialist machining),
  • Medical Devices (patient-specific items outsourced for sterile packaging),
  • Electronics (customer-specific assemblies outsourced for specialist coating),
  • Apparel & Fashion (sales-order fabric sent to CMT vendor for cut-make-trim).

What changed in 2608:

  • The Subcontracting PO can now contain BOM with Components both from Regular inventory stock and Special stock (Sales Order Stock: E) in the same order.
  •  No stock type conversion required: E transfers directly to F at the vendor.
  • Full MTO support: materials and costs tied strictly to the individual customer sales order no cross-consumption.
  • No financial document at Goods Issue: the FI/accounting posting occurs only at Goods Receipt.

For scope item 7DW, the stock type transitions are:

  • Sales Order Stock component (e.g. RM027): E (at producer)  →  F Stock (at vendor)  →  Consumed on GR
  • Regular Plant Stock component: O (at producer)  →  O at vendor  →  Consumed on GR
  • Finished product (e.g. SG035): Received back into Sales Order Stock (E) on GR

Important: No financial or accounting document is generated when components are issued to the subcontractor (Goods Issue). This is a stock-to-stock transfer only no consumption has occurred yet. The financial posting is triggered only at Goods Receipt.

  • Example Master Data: SG035(main material), Components: RM027(Sales Order Stock)
  • In the material Master of RM027: Go to MRP4 and set the individual/Collective requirement as 1(Individual)
  • Note: The Individual/Collective Requirement = 1 (Individual) setting on MRP4 is mandatory. Without it, MRP will not plan the component against the specific sales order it will be treated as pooled stock and the Sales Order Stock (E) link will be lost.

  • For SG035: In the MRP2 Tab: maintain the following.

  • Go to the tab MRP4 and set the individual/Collective requirement as 1(Individual)

Process Steps:

Step 1: Create Sales Order

  • Create a Sales Order in the Manage Sales Order App.

  • Ensure the Storage Location, shipping point is determined rightly
  • Save the Sales Order document and note down the number.

Step 2: Ensure Components are Available

  • Before running MRP, ensure the required components are available in the correct stock type:
  • Note: Either procure the components via a standard purchase order or production order referencing the sales order (so they land in Sales Order Stock E), or ensure sufficient stock already exists in the respective stock type before planning. Do not start MRP planning until the component stock is confirmed available.

Step 3: Run MRP

  • Schedule MRP Run in the background in the App: Schedule MRP Run Or
  • Go to the App: Manage Material Coverage and run the MRP directly
  • Select the Material and click on details and Run MRP directly here.  Post MRP Run, for the respective Sales Order, a Purchase Requisition should be created. Note down the Purchase Requisition number.

Step 4: Create Subcontracting Purchase Order

  • Go to the App: Process Purchase Requistions.
  • Enter the Purchase Requisition or Filter based on your criteria, Select the Purchase Requisitions and Assign the Supplier, should be determined based on the Purchase InfoRecords.

  • Then Click Create Purchase Order, The Purchase Order Simulation should be displayed.
  • In the Items Table, the Account Assignment = M (Sales Order) and Item Category = L (Subcontracting) must be pre-determined. Note: Account Assignment M = Make-to-Order / Sales Order. This ties the subcontracting PO to the specific customer sales order, ensuring all costs are tracked at order level. Do not change this value.

  • Select the Item, in the Material Data tab, click on components.

  • The Special Stock should be set for RM027 as E

  • Go to Account Assignment tab: Sales Order information should be determined.

  • In the Confirmation Tab, select Inbound Delivery under the Confirmation Control.
  • Save the Purchase Order and note down the number.

Step 5: Post Goods Issue to Subcontractor

  • Go to the app Subcontracting Cockpit (F2948) and enter the Purchase Order and click Go, the components details should be displayed.

  • Select the components and click Expand all

  • Then select all the components and click Create Delivery.

  • Ensure all the details are determined, For example for RM027, the Special indicator is E, Sales Order and Its Item number should be displayed

  • Then click Create Delivery. An outbound delivery document should be created, note down the number.
  • Login as Inventory Manager and Check the Stock in the App: Display Stock Overview before posting goods issue.
  • Enter the material name SG035, Plant: 1010, Storage location as 101B and click execute

  • Enter Material as RM027, Plant 1010 and Storage loc as 101C and execute

  • Click Detailed display, The stock associated to respective Sales Order should be displayed.

  • Login as Shipping Specialist, go the App: Change Outbound Delivery.
  • Enter the outbound delivery document, which was created in the previous step, go to picking tab and complete the picking process and save the document.
  • Click post goods issue
  • Go to The Document flow of the Outbound Delivery and click on the material document which was created, check the below:
  • For RM027: The Special stock indicator should be E at the Producer and F once moved under Subcontractor’s Stock

  •  Important: No financial/accounting document is generated at Goods Issue, this is a stock-to-stock transfer only. No consumption has occurred yet. The FI/accounting document is generated only at Goods Receipt (Step 8).
  • Financial Document is not generated from outbound delivery, because it's only transfer the material from E stock to Subcontracting stock, no consumption has occurred yet.

Step 6 :Verify Stock at Subcontractor

  • Login as Inventory Manager.
  • You can as well check the stocks of each material in the App: Display stock overview. And see the stock details, how many are with subcontracting vendor and how many with Producer. For example:

 

Step 7: Receive Inbound Delivery from Subcontractor

  • Login as Receiving Specialist and Go to App: Change Inbound Deliveries f and enter the Inbounde Delivery number and Click ENTER
  • Select the Item and go to the tab: Stock Placement, Enter the Putaway Qty and click Save.

Step 8: Post Goods Receipt

 Note: The Goods Receipt is the trigger for the financial posting. The finished product SG035 is received into Sales Order Stock (E) and the subcontracting fee + material costs are posted against the customer sales order.

  • Go to the App: Post Goods Receipt for Inbound Delivery and Enter the Inbound delivery number which was created in the previous steps.
  • Check the Special Stock type as E and Sales Order and its item number information are predetermined.

  • Select the Item and check the details. Check the component details are reflected rightly with respective special stock type for the component Material RM027

  • Post the Goods Receipt and note down the material document.

Step 9: Verify Material Document and Journal Entry

Doc Info tab → FI Documents → Journal Entry

  • Check the Material Document, following details should be available and Accounting documents should be generated.

  • Click Doc Info tab and click FI Documents

  • Check the Journal Entry:

Step 10: Create Supplier Invoice

  • Note: this is optional because we are focusing on the logistic process

Step 11: Final Stock Verification

  • Check the Stock in the App: Display Stock Overview

Summary:

Scope item 7DW closes a critical gap in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition by enabling full MTO subcontracting with Sales Order Stock (E). Make-to-Order manufacturers can now outsource operations while maintaining complete sales order integrity, preventing cross-consumption, and ensuring accurate order-level cost tracking.

entry.title: Re: SAP Cloud Connector cannot connect to SAP BTP subaccount
entry.content:
Seen this "Preparation of tunnel certificate failed" error a couple times, it's usually one of a few specific things rather than a random glitch.
First thing worth checking is whether the JVM being used by Cloud Connector has an updated root certificate store. SAP switched some of their backend hosts to DigiCert TLS RSA4096 Root G5 and DigiCert Global Root G3 a while back, and if your Cloud Connector's Java runtime is on an older version, it won't recognize the new certificate chain, which causes tunnel setup to fail right at the certificate preparation step. Worth checking the scc_core.trc trace file for anything mentioning "PKIX path building failed" or "unable to find valid certification path", that confirms it's a certificate trust issue rather than credentials or network.
Second thing, double check the subaccount ID and region in the tunnel URL actually match what's shown in your BTP cockpit. Sometimes people copy an old subaccount GUID or the wrong region host (like cf.us10 vs cf.eu10) and the tunnel prep fails silently instead of giving a clear "wrong account" error.
Third, if it's not a certificate or ID mismatch, it could genuinely be a firewall/proxy blocking outbound access to the specific hosts Cloud Connector needs for that region. Worth testing basic connectivity to the hana.ondemand.com host directly from the machine running Cloud Connector, not just assuming general internet access is enough, since some corporate firewalls whitelist by domain and miss newer SAP hosts.
If updating the JVM to the latest supported version doesn't fix it, I'd open an SAP ticket with the full scc_core.trc attached, since tunnel certificate failures tied to backend cert rotations usually need SAP to confirm which hosts changed on their side.

entry.title: Re: How to create a non-valued Purch.Requisition in a TPP...
entry.content:
This is pretty standard at the moment unfortunately, SAP's response would be that the sales team should know the purchase price because they wouldn't know if the transaction was profitable otherwise.  There is no free of charge purchase requisition functionality that I am aware of.

The moving average price of the product will never get updated using item category CB1.  This is because the transactions alway post the customer stock segment.

I have found it helps to set the product valuation price to a minimal value (0.01) to get a requisition generated and the sales order complete.  The procurement team need to be aware of but you need to ensure that the valuation is corrected either manually or by selecting a contract item or purchasing information record.

entry.title: Fintech Professional transitioning to SAP GRC – Learning Path & Sandbox Guidance
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I'm Prateek Kumar, a Senior Manager with 7+ years of experience in the Indian fintech and payments industry. I'm now making a deliberate transition into SAP GRC consulting and would love guidance from this community.

MY BACKGROUND -

I hold a B.Tech in Computer Science (2016), My entire career has been in regulated fintech environments, working across companies like One97 Communications, Cashfree Payments etc. My core domain expertise includes:

• KYC / AML Compliance – Designing and executing customer due diligence processes aligned with RBI and PMLA guidelines
• Fraud Monitoring & Risk Management – Building fraud detection frameworks, SLA-driven ops, escalation matrices
• Merchant Onboarding & Risk – End-to-end onboarding, risk profiling, and ongoing merchant monitoring
• Regulatory Compliance – Working within RBI's Payment Aggregator framework, audit readiness, control documentation
• Team Leadership – Managing teams of 30+ across operations, compliance, and risk functions

MY GOAL

I'm targeting to learn SAP GRC along with the certification of SAP GRC Access Control by September/October 2026, with the goal of moving into a GRC consulting role (Risk Advisory) at a Big 4 or top-tier consulting firm.

WHAT I'M SEEKING FROM THIS COMMUNITY

1. Learning Path - I'm starting from scratch on SAP GRC.

What is the most effective step-by-step learning sequence you'd recommend? Specifically looking for:
• Free resources (YouTube channels, SAP Community blogs, learning.sap.com content)
• Budget-friendly courses (Udemy or similar) that include sandbox/practice system access
• Any specific study plan or sequence.

2. Sandbox Access - Free or affordable SAP GRC 12.0 practice system where I can do hands-on configuration (ARA, EAM, ARM, BRM). 

3. Connect with Career Transitioners - If you've made a similar transition (from a fintech background into SAP GRC consultant), would love to connect and learn from your journey.

Thanks in advance for your time and insights. Any guidance, even a single tip, is deeply appreciated!
Prateek.

entry.title: Re: Catalogs and Groups for FIORI Launchpad
entry.content:
Hello,

Catalogs and Groups are still technically present in S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 but are officially deprecated and will be removed in a future version.
SAP Note 3500249 explicitly confirms that Groups will still be available with SAP S/4HANA 2025 and will not be removed with SAP S/4HANA 2025.
3500249 - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP S/4HANA: Replacement of Groups (deprecated) by Spaces and Pages

Deprecation status: The Groups-based home page is officially deprecated (since S/4HANA 2021 FPS01); SAP no longer invests in innovations for Groups, and a final removal version/date is not yet determined but will be in a release higher than S/4HANA 2023.

You can proceed with the upgrade to S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 while still on Groups — Groups will technically still be present.
However, you must complete the Technical Catalog Migration as part of the upgrade itself, and you should plan the Groups → Spaces & Pages migration as a post-upgrade project with appropriate resourcing and user change management.

Best regards,
Joe

entry.title: Teaching SAP Without Teaching SAP
entry.content:
Whether it's at conferences, Train-the-Trainer workshops, or conversations with colleagues, two questions come up more often than any others:

How do I fit SAP into an already crowded curriculum?

How do I justify teaching a specific software platform when there are countless other platforms students could learn?

To be honest, I have never really approached SAP this way.

From the first time I incorporated SAP into my teaching, my objective was not to teach students SAP. The objective was to teach accounting, business processes, controls, analytics, and information systems concepts using an enterprise system that reflected how organisations actually operate, while giving students hands-on experience applying what they were learning.

Quite simply, I thought it was odd that students could study information systems, business processes, and enterprise technology without ever experiencing an enterprise system themselves.

Instead of asking:

How or why do we teach SAP?

I have always found it more useful to ask:

How can SAP help us teach the concepts we already need students to learn?

I think the distinction is important.

Of course, there are contexts where learning SAP itself is the goal, particularly when supporting certification pathways or specialised training.

For many university subjects, however, our objective is broader.

Whether students are studying accounting, information systems, business analytics, supply chain management, data science, software engineering, computer science, or another discipline, the goal is rarely to learn a particular software platform in isolation. The goal is to understand concepts, solve problems, develop professional judgement, and build capabilities that remain valuable long after today's technologies evolve.

We want graduates who can understand complex systems, work with data, solve real-world problems, and apply their knowledge in technology-enabled environments. Depending on the discipline, that might involve designing solutions, analysing information, improving processes, understanding organisations, supporting decision-making, creating software, or driving innovation.

In that context, SAP becomes a means to an end rather than the end itself.

One principle that has guided my thinking is:

Teach concepts first. Use SAP to make those concepts visible.

Students do not need another piece of software to memorise. What they need is an opportunity to understand how organisations actually operate and how technology supports that operation.

While I have focused on ERP systems in this post, the same philosophy applies more broadly across the SAP ecosystem.

If I am teaching business processes, process analysis, or process improvement, tools such as SAP Signavio can help students visualise, analyse, and improve processes rather than simply learning about process mapping in theory.

If I am teaching human resource management, SAP SuccessFactors can help students understand how organisations manage workforce data, talent, performance, and employee development in practice.

If I am teaching analytics, SAP Analytics Cloud can help students explore how organisations transform data into information and information into insight (Business Builders is a particularly good example of this approach).

The technology may change, but the philosophy remains the same.

Teach concepts first. Use technology to make those concepts visible, relevant, and authentic.

For me, and the subjects I teach, this means using SAP to help students connect theory with practice, gain experience, and make abstract concepts more visible. A few examples include:

Supporting Decision Making Through Integrated Information

One concept I teach is how organisations use information to support decision-making.

I could explain that ERP systems collect data from across an organisation, integrate information from different functional areas, and provide decision makers with the information they need to manage the business.

Instead, students experience this first-hand through ERPsim.

As they participate in a dynamic business simulation, students see how operational decisions generate data, how that data flows through the enterprise system, and how integrated information supports future decisions. Rather than being told how an ERP system supports decision-making, they experience the consequences of those decisions in real time.

The learning objective is not to teach students what SAP does. The learning objective is to help them understand how information supports decision-making in organisations. ERPsim simply provides an environment where they can experience that process for themselves.

Interestingly, I have found the same approach works well beyond the classroom. I have used ERPsim with CEOs of multi-billion-dollar organisations and senior military leaders, including two-star generals. The value comes from experiencing integrated decision-making in action rather than learning the mechanics of an ERP system.

Understanding Controls and Governance

Another example comes from teaching controls and governance.

Students often work through the S/4HANA Global Bike Inc. (GBI) case study with access that would never be granted to a single employee in a real organisation. Initially, this is simply a practical way to allow them to experience an end-to-end business process.

What makes the exercise powerful is what happens next.

As students work through the case, they begin to realise they are performing activities that would normally be undertaken by different people across the organisation. By returning to the case materials, they can see the different roles and individuals involved at each step of the process and compare them with the level of access they have been given within SAP.

This naturally leads to questions such as:

  • Whose role am I performing at this step?
  • Why am I switching between different roles?
  • Why are approvals required?
  • Why are different people involved in the process?

Rather than starting with a lecture on segregation of duties and internal controls, students discover the problem themselves through experience.

The discussion then shifts from understanding the process to asking:

How could an organisation enforce this in practice?

Once students reach that point, controls, governance, approvals, role design, and access management stop being abstract concepts. They become practical solutions to a problem the students have already experienced first-hand.

Again, the objective is not to teach SAP security. The objective is to help students understand why organisations implement controls and governance mechanisms in the first place.

Understanding Professional Practice and Career Opportunities

A third example comes from the way SAP helps connect students with industry.

The learning objective is not to teach students about SAP careers. The learning objective is to help students understand how the concepts they learning are applied in practice and expose them to career opportunities they may never have previously considered.

Through the SAP ecosystem, I can bring guest speakers into the classroom to share their industry experience. Many studied accounting before moving into consulting, transformation, analytics, governance, or technology-focused roles.

Their stories help students see how the concepts they learning are applied in real organisations.

For accounting students in particular, these conversations are often eye-opening. Many begin their studies expecting that an accounting career will primarily involve compliance, reporting, or audit. Instead, they discover careers that combine business knowledge, technology, process improvement, analytics, and enterprise systems.

The guest speakers bring the subject to life in a way that no textbook or lecture can.

SAP makes these connections possible, but SAP itself is not the learning objective. The learning objective is helping students understand why what they are learning matters and where it may take them after graduation.

Perhaps the biggest benefit of this approach is that students often develop strong SAP skills even when SAP is not positioned as the primary learning objective.

Rather than following a set of instructions to complete a transaction, they are using the system to understand a process, investigate a problem, analyse information, or answer a business question.

The learning becomes purposeful.

The result is that students develop both conceptual understanding and practical enterprise system experience.

In many ways, they learn SAP because they are learning something else. And perhaps that is how I have always justified SAP in the curriculum.

So How Did I Justify Teaching SAP?

That answer above helped answer the second question I posed at the beginning of this post:

How do I justify teaching a particular software platform when there are so many others available?

The honest answer is that my initial interest in SAP was similar to that of many academics. SAP's market presence, industry adoption, graduate opportunities, and the broader ecosystem made it worth exploring.

When I was offered a free trial through a University Competence Centre (UCC) and ERPsim Lab, I saw an opportunity to give students experience with an enterprise system and decided to see what they would learn from it. At the end of the trial, I was asked to prepare a proposal to justify ongoing investment.

Around the same time, one of my students submitted a reflection on what she had learned from the experience. What struck me was not what she wrote about SAP, but how she connected ideas from across multiple subjects and linked them back to what she had experienced.

After reading it, I realised her reflection explained the value of the experience far better than anything I had written.

So I tore up my proposal and submitted her reflection instead.

The answer was a rapid yes.

For me, that reinforced an important lesson. Market share, industry demand, certifications, and employability may have been what started the conversation. They were not what ultimately justified continuing it.

The value was never the software itself. The value was what students experienced, understood, and achieved because of it.

In many ways, they learn SAP because they are learning something else.

I'd love to hear examples from others in the SAP community.

How have you used SAP to help students experience the concepts you teach?

Whether it is accounting, analytics, business processes, supply chain management, finance, sustainability, data science, software engineering, computer science, or another area, I would love to hear what has worked particularly well for your students.

And yes, I fully appreciate the irony that I'm posting this on a SAP forum, regularly attend SAP events around the world, run SAP Train-the-Trainer workshops, and serve as President of a SAP University Alliances Academic Board.

But I still don't think I teach SAP. 😄

Matt Dyki

Teaching Fellow, The University of Melbourne

President, SAP University Alliances Asia Pacific Academic Board

entry.title: Clarification on Standard Processing of Multiple DELFOR IDocs
entry.content:
The sender system (Infineon) and the receiver system (Bosch) are both SAP systems.

Infineon sends Supplier Confirmations for the same Scheduling Agreement in two separate DELFOR IDocs (one containing Firm schedule lines and another containing Forecast schedule lines) in DELFOR D.97A/D.04A format.

We would like to understand the standard SAP behavior for processing these multiple inbound DELFOR IDocs. Specifically, how should SAP process Supplier Confirmations received in separate IDocs for the same Scheduling Agreement.

entry.title: Re: Structured address data elements in ISO 20022 messages
entry.content:
Dear @jithendrashiva ,
on ECC 618 we do not have dedicated fields for structured addresses in FPAYH/FPAYHX/FPAYP structures. Therefore in our CGI solution we are reading addresses into reference fields in Event 05 of the Payment Media Workbench. Please refer to the note https://me.sap.com/notes/2513995/E to get all the reference fields. Addresses are on the tab GENERIC as these are available in all country versions.

In case you are not using our CGI format but rather your own, you need to read addresses from ADRC table (in event 05 or 06) and map them accordingly.
Kind regards,
Ondrej
